title: Project Overview / About Us - HBCU Digital Library Trust
text: About Us Courtesy of North Carolina Central University Archives Mission The HBCU Digital Library Trust is building capacity with Historically Black Colleges and Universities to digitally preserve and provide global access to their archival collections, sustain institutional, cultural, and community memory, and ensure stories are discovered, maintained, remembered and told. Vision With trust at the forefront, we acknowledge and celebrate the undeniable lived experiences and legacy of Black people by being the preeminent digital repository network of HBCU archival collections. title: Project Overview / About Us - HBCU Digital Library Trust
text: Jelani Favors United Negro College Fund Jordan Ross University of Pennsylvania Jennifer Ferretti After Violence Project Crystal Sanders Emory University Janice Franklin Alabama State University Marquis Taylor Northwestern University Coleman A. Jordan Morgan State University Adrienne Webber Grambling State University HBCU Library Alliance Doretha Williams Smithsonian Institute, National Museum of African American History and Culture History In 2006, the HBCU Library Alliance and Cornell University Library collaborated on an innovative, Andrew W. Mellon Foundation grant-funded initiative to prepare library staff from Historically Black Colleges and Universities to produce digital collections, and develop an Internet-based, searchable database of HBCU library materials. The initiative became one of the first in the nation to build a national framework for digitization of collaborative HBCU library cultural heritage materials. Since 2008, the HBCU Digital Collection platform has been hosted by the Atlanta University Center (AUC) Robert W. Woodruff Library, and is heavily utilized for research, new scholarship, teaching and learning.* With a shared goal to deepen capacity and advance open, public access to African American archives and special collections, the HBCU Library Alliance and Harvard Library began partnering in 2023. With funds provided by the Harvard & the Legacy of Slavery Initiative, they developed the HBCU Digital Library Trust to expand existing services and business models to scale up and strengthen capacity for the digitization, discovery, and preservation of more HBCU collections. In addition to hosting the digital collection platform, the AUC Woodruff Library provides robust services for contributing HBCUs as a Digitization Hub. *Adapted from the HBCU Library Alliance – Cornell University Library Digitization Initiative website. title: Partnerships - HBCU Digital Library Trust
text: Partnerships Courtesy of North Carolina Central University Archives Building Strong Partnerships for a Shared Mission Partnerships are at the core of the HBCU Digital Library Trust project. We recognize the immense value of collaboration in achieving our mission to digitize, discover, and preserve African American history collections. These partnerships are instrumental in amplifying our impact and advancing open access to these invaluable resources. Our Key Collaborators and Allies Our project thrives on the support, contributions and expertise of key organizations. Here are the organizations that play a vital role in our mission: Harvard Library aims to be global leaders in expanding world knowledge and intellectual exploration. Their efforts are motivated and powered by working collaboratively, embracing diverse perspectives, championing access, aiming for the extraordinary, and always leading with curiosity. LEARN MORE The HBCU Library Alliance is a consortium that supports the collaboration of information professionals dedicated to providing an array of resources designed to strengthen Historically Black Colleges and Universities and their constituents. text: Library / LibGuides / HBCU Digital Library Trust- Guide for Contributors / Metadata Guide HBCU Digital Library Trust- Guide for Contributors: Metadata Guide This guide provides an overview and information for HBCU Digital Library Trust project contributors. Project Overview Scanning Hub Team Digitization Process Metadata Guide Shipping and Packing Collection Use Statistics Metadata Guide This document provides recommendations on formatting and usage when creating descriptive metadata for digital objects included in the HBCU Digital Library Trust collections. Metadata for the HBCU Digital Library Trust is based on the Dublin Core metadata schema and OCLC FAST subject headings. Dublin Core consists of a series of repeatable elements identified as basic descriptive information for electronic resources. The HBCU Digital Library Trust strives to create descriptions that are respectful, sensitive, and accurate. Whenever possible we consult best practices and work collaboratively with participants. The guide can viewed here or downloaded as a PDF document.  Metadata Guide Search this Guide Search 6/14/26, 4:02 PM Metadata Guide - HBCU Digital Library Trust- Guide for Contributors - LibGuides at Atlanta University Center - Robert W. title: Metadata Guide - HBCU Digital Library Trust Guide for Contributors
filename: Metadata Guide - HBCU Digital Library Trust- Guide for Contributors - LibGuides at Atlanta University Center - Robert W. Woodruff Library.pdf
text: Dublin Core Field Descriptions ­Dublin Core ContentDM Field Description DC.Title: searchable, public/staff field; required field Title Title is a concise sentence which will be used to label the object; it may also be the first line of the descriptive information. Use existing titles when available. in the case of newspaper articles or other work when an exact title is being used, put that title in quotes, example: "Students Celebrate Homecoming Weekend", October 1966. If no title is provided, use the information within the document or a finding aid if provided. Avoid using abbreviations. If no title is available, supply a brief and descriptive title, e.g., Jesse Jackson Speaks in an Auditorium, circa 1995. Use title case style capitalization, e.g., The Scholarly Communications Crisis, and make it read smoothly. If unsure of what to capitalize, use a generator such as https://titlecase.com/ or https://titlecaseconverter.com/ EXAMPLES: [*note punctuation for dates]: Jesse Jackson Speaks in an Auditorium, January 5, 1995 Jesse Jackson Speaks in an Auditorium, January 1995 Jesse Jackson Speaks in an Auditorium, 1995 Jesse Jackson Speaks in an Auditorium, circa 1995 DC.Creator: searchable, public/staff field REPEATABLE Author/Creator A repeatable field with the delimiter “;”, the creator is the author of a document, the person who took a photograph, the person being interviewed for an oral history, etc. Information about the creator should be taken from the original document or the Finding Aid or the catalog record. Record the name as: Last name, First name. If not in the LoC name authority headings, record following the convention of LastName, FirstName MI. EXAMPLES: Leming, James S., 1941- Shakur, Tupac, 1971-1996 Smith, Robert A. Smith, Robert A.; Walker, Jane DC.Date: Searchable, public/staff field; required field Date.Original The date, or publication date, refers to the date the original was created, not the scan. This field is machine- readable and needs to be entered in YYYY, YYYY-MM, or YYYY-MM-DD format. If there s no known date (where you have given a “circa” date in the title, include a limited range of dates around the date you approximated. For example, if you think item is “circa 1965” the range in this date field could be 1963-1968 which would then date that object as 1963; 1964; 1965; 1966; 1967; 1968 for searching purposes. EXAMPLES: 1987 1987-10 1987-10-31 1963-1968 DC.Description: searchable, public/staff field Description In photographs and other archival materials, a short description of the item. The description field for a photograph or document is a shorter abstract, usually one or two sentences, with common grammar and punctuation, and avoiding personal or editorial statements. Proof your work to ensure all sentences are well-formed and complete. Include all descriptive keywords you may think researchers would find important. If a .jpeg/photograph with handwriting with descriptive writing on front or back of photograph, you can include this here to make it searchable using “Written on recto:” or “Written on verso:” to refer to writing on the front or back of image. EXAMPLES: Members of the Early Bennett College Seminary Classes held in the St. Matthews Church Basement. Annie Merner Pfeiffer Hall was erected in 1934 as a senior dormitory under the 1930's building program begun after Bennett College became a women's college. The building was designed by Charles C. Hartmann, one of Greensboro's most prominent architects. 6/14/26, 4:02 PM Metadata Guide - HBCU Digital Library Trust- Guide for Contributors - LibGuides at Atlanta University Center - Robert W. Woodruff Library https://research.auctr.edu/c.php?g=1451206&p=10786542 3/6

title: Metadata Guide - HBCU Digital Library Trust Guide for Contributors
filename: Metadata Guide - HBCU Digital Library Trust- Guide for Contributors - LibGuides at Atlanta University Center - Robert W. Woodruff Library.pdf
text: Last Updated: Mar 11, 2026 12:00 PM URL: https://research.auctr.edu/HBCU-DLT  Print Page Login to LibApps Subjects: Archives Research Center & Exhibits, Scholarly Communication, Open Access and Open Education Resources Exterior of Bibb Graves Hall with students gathering outside on benches. Written on verso: Bib Graves Hall was built in 1928 as the dormitory for women on the Montgomery campus. DC.Subject: searchable, public/staff field REPEATABLE Subjects A repeatable field with the delimiter “;”, FAST (Faceted Application of Subject Terminology) is derived from the Library of Congress Subject Headings (LCSH), one of the library domain’s most widely used subject terminology schemas. FAST has been developed in large part to meet the need for a general-use subject terminology scheme, which is simple to learn and apply: https://fast.oclc.org/searchfast/ Each object should have 3-5 subjects -- just enough to describe the main subjects of the object. Copy and paste terms to avoid misspelling and to keep capitalization exactly as listed. Best practice is to create, retain, and use your own “controlled vocabulary” using FAST, so like materials in your collection will have consistent tagging. If no appropriate subject in FAST, create your own, just be consistent. Note that these are TOPICAL terms from FAST, not corporate or personal names, geographical or named events. These should either go in description or appropriate field. For more information on FAST, see the FAST Quickstart Guide. See end of Guide for a starter list of FAST subjects for the HBCU collections. REPEATABLE: Buildings and grounds; Campus life; African American universities and colleges EXAMPLES – FAST terms: African American architects African American civic leaders African American student movements African American students African American universities and colleges College student newspapers and periodicals Political participation Theology--Study and teaching EXAMPLES – Non-FAST terms (may be most suitable for school collections): Buildings and grounds Campus life Commencement ceremonies Events and programs Faculty and staff Groups and organizations Portraits and people Presidents DC.Type: searchable, public/staff field; required field Object Type Denotes genre or type of material. If more than one term is applicable, list the form of the item scanned first. Terms in this field are taken from the LC Thesaurus for Graphic Materials: Genre and Physical Characteristic Terms (LC TGM II) EXAMPLES: Black & white photographs [includes 1900s sepia toned photographs] Color photographs Lithographs Clippings Periodicals 6/14/26, 4:02 PM Metadata Guide - HBCU Digital Library Trust- Guide for Contributors - LibGuides at Atlanta University Center - Robert W. Woodruff Library https://research.auctr.edu/c.php?g=1451206&p=10786542 4/6

text: Manuscripts Documents Postcards Ephemera DC.Format Format The format of the image or digital file, expressed in the MIME format following type/subtype convention. Enter the access copy MIME Type as it is displayed. Mozilla gives a nicer truncated list. These will be the common formats, but note that there may be other subtypes used dependent on the format. EXAMPLES: Most used type vocabulary: image/jpeg [an access photo jpeg file] application/pdf [an access pdf file] text/plain [a human readable document] DC: Coverage-Spatial Geographic Location The Location of the image, audiovisual event, or publication – use OCLC FAST for consistent display. Note that a general convention in FAST is BroaderTerm--NarrowerTerm--EvenNarrowerTerm. If location is not in FAST and needs to be created, please follow that convention. [note: separator is TWO DASHES WITH NO SPACING IN BETWEEN ELEMENTS] GENERALLY, this will only be state--city or the like depending on where in the world the location is. Only in rare occasions would a third refinement be used. If not found in FAST, Geography information can always be placed in the description/abstract. *if no location can be discerned, leave blank. EXAMPLES: Liberia--Monrovia Georgia--Atlanta North Carolina--Charlotte DC.Identifier: searchable, public/staff field Identifier Number that users can reference to inquire about the digital image, typically the file name of the object. EXAMPLES: auc.001.0307.jpg vhi.btw.ph014.jpg vsu.arc.dee.txt001.jpg DC.Source: non- searchable, public/staff field Repository The institution where the item is physically located. EXAMPLES: Grambling State University Southern University at Shreveport Library DC.Relation-Is Part of: searchable, public/staff field Repository Collection This field contains the collection name and its number, represented as a phrase. EXAMPLES: William E. Meed Collection. PH Coll 246 Atlanta University Photographs Collection, auc.001 Joseph Willams Papers, MU_012 DC. Language: searchable, public/staff field REPEATABLE Language A language of the intellectual content of the resource. Values should be taken from the ISO 639-2 standard, and expressed as three letter codes (i.e., eng, fre). https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_ISO_639-2_codes 6/14/26, 4:02 PM Metadata Guide - HBCU Digital Library Trust- Guide for Contributors - LibGuides at Atlanta University Center - Robert W. title: FAQs - HBCU Digital Library Trust
text: FAQs Courtesy of Lincoln University of Missouri Archives 1. My HBCU did not previously contribute to the HBCU Library Alliance Digital Collection. Is it possible to participate in the HBCU Digital Library Trust Initiative at this time? Yes, all HBCUs are eligible to participate in the Trust initiative to expand the digital collection whether or not they previously contributed. 2. What is Harvard Library’s connection to the HBCU Digital Library Trust? Harvard Library is pleased to be in partnership with the HBCU Library Alliance with the shared goal of advancing open, public access to archives and special collections pertaining to African American history. In addition to nearly $6 million which has been designated to support the Trust through the Harvard & the Legacy of Slavery initiative, Harvard Library staff will also contribute administrative resources, training, and best practices to aid in the project’s objectives. 3. Will my HBCU institution retain copyright and ownership of any digitized materials contributed to the HBCU Digital Library Trust collection? Yes! All copyright and/or ownership of archival materials and digital derivatives will be retained by the respective HBCU. Digitized materials will be openly accessible to anyone with an internet connection. A statement providing contact information for researchers wishing to request permission to publish from the HBCU will continue to be found within the metadata for digital objects. text: Home > About > News & Events > The AUC Woodruff Library Awarded $500,000 Grant from the MacArthur Foundation to Expand HBCU Digitization Project Featured News The AUC Woodruff Library Awarded $500,000 Grant from the MacArthur Foundation to Expand HBCU Digitization Project September 4, 2025 The Atlanta University Center (AUC) Robert W. Woodruff Library has been awarded a $500,000 grant from the MacArthur Foundation. This significant funding will bolster the library’s efforts to expand access to valuable resources while enhancing the digitization and preservation of Historically Black Colleges and Universities’ (HBCUs) collections. This grant marks an important milestone, adding momentum to an existing multi-million, multi-year collaborative project, the HBCU Digital Library Trust, which was initiated in 2023 with support from Harvard & the Legacy of Slavery Initiative. The HBCU Digital Library Trust, a collaborative with a shared goal to deepen capacity and advance open, public access to African American archives and special collections, partners closely with HBCUs, the AUC Woodruff Library, the HBCU Library Alliance, and Harvard Library. Together, they sustain the capacity for the digitization, discovery, and preservation of African American history collections held by HBCU libraries and archives. 6/14/26, 5:17 PM The AUC Woodruff Library Awarded $500,000 Grant from the MacArthur Foundation to Expand HBCU Digitization Project - Robert W. title: AUC Woodruff Library Awarded $500,000 Grant from MacArthur Foundation
filename: The AUC Woodruff Library Awarded $500,000 Grant from the MacArthur Foundation to Expand HBCU Digitization Project - Robert W. Woodruff Library_ Atlanta University Center (AUC) Library.pdf
text: Dr. Mary McLeod Bethune Leading Graduating Students, circa 1940, Bethune-Cookman University Digital Collection From this collaboration, the AUC Woodruff Library hosts the HBCU Library Alliance Digital Library portal, providing robust services for contributing HBCUs and hosting the digital collection platform. The digital portal includes archival materials from over 30 HBCUs, with a growing aim to digitize holdings from all of the 107 HBCUs located across 21 states, the District of Columbia, and the U.S. Virgin Islands. Since 2024, 16 HBCUs have either reconnected with or newly joined the HBCU digital collection repository via the HBCU Digital Library Trust. Furthermore, in 2024 alone, the digital portal has stored over 16,500 digitized items—including photographs, documents, and audiovisual recordings. The new two-year investment from the MacArthur Foundation will deepen and sustain the capacity of HBCU libraries and archives to digitize, preserve, and globally share collections integral to African American history and culture. Emphasizing sustainability, this extended project will enable more institutions within the HBCU Library Alliance, in partnership with the HBCU Digital Library Trust, to access training, infrastructure, and support necessary for long-term digital stewardship. “The MacArthur Foundation’s generous grant is a transformative next step for this vital work,” said Dr. Kimberley Bugg, CEO and Library Director of the AUC Woodruff Library. “It not only sustains but also expands our ability to provide global access to HBCU collections, ensuring that these critical narratives are preserved and shared with the world. We are committed to building an enduring infrastructure for digital preservation that reflects the depth, breadth, and richness of African American heritage.” Adding to this sentiment, Andrea Jackson Gavin, Program Director for HBCU Digital Library Trust, stated, “It is an honor for our team to work with HBCUs to enhance virtual access and provide digital preservation, which we know is vital to supporting new scholarship, and long- term safeguarding of these unique collections. This support from the MacArthur Foundation represents the continuity of such important measures for our HBCU institutions.” This extension underscores a shared commitment to equity, education, and inclusive historical preservation. As the project embarks on its next phase, it aims to serve as a model for sustainable digital access and partnership-driven cultural stewardship. 6/14/26, 5:17 PM The AUC Woodruff Library Awarded $500,000 Grant from the MacArthur Foundation to Expand HBCU Digitization Project - Robert W. text: About the Atlanta University Center Robert W. Woodruff Library Established in 1982, the Atlanta University Center (AUC) Robert W. Woodruff Library is named in honor of the late Robert Winship Woodruff, a philanthropist and former CEO of The Coca-Cola Company. The library serves three historically black colleges and universities (HBCUs) – Clark Atlanta University, Morehouse College, and Spelman College. Beyond its aesthetic appeal, this state-of-the-art facility has become a leading repository of information resources and an innovator in the delivery of digital content. The AUC Woodruff Library also houses the Archives Research Center, renowned for its extensive collection of materials related to the African American experience. This includes the Morehouse College Martin Luther King, Jr. Collection, as well as the Joseph Echols Lowery and Evelyn Gibson Lowery Collection. For the HBCU Digital Library Trust, the Digital Yard is an online gathering place where the history and archives of HBCUs are celebrated, remembered, preserved, and shared. That gathering place, the HBCU Digital Collection, which is an online repository you can access 24 hours a day, 7 days a week, anywhere where there is internet capability. As we take this hour to share more information about the HBCU Digital Library Trust with you, we acknowledge having many different audiences today in the digital yard. title: HBCU Digital Collection Research Guide: Conducting Research
text: HBCU Digital Collection Research Guide: Conducting Research Skip to Main Content 1. LibraryLibGuidesHBCU Digital Collection Research GuideConducting Research Library LibGuides HBCU Digital Collection Research Guide Conducting Research Search this Guide HBCU Digital Collection Research Guide: Conducting Research Welcome About the HBCU Digital Collection and the HBCU Digital Library Trust Conducting Research Video Tutorial: How to Research the HBCU Digital Collection Copyright Basics Project Processes HBCU Digital Collection Research Guide: Conducting Research 1

title: HBCU Digital Collection Research Guide: Conducting Research
text: Research Tips For a full description of the general research process and helpful information please see the Atlanta University Center Robert W. Woodruff Library "Doing Research" LibGuide. Things to Keep in Mind Materials such as yearbooks have intrinsic research value, and they along with other prevalent materials are described below. Please see the "Themes Within the HBCU Digital Collection" section of this page to explore ways in which these materials can be applied in research. If you need to save materials, keep a personal record of the links because you are unable to save links in CONTENTdm (defined in Project Processes section) To create citations for your research please use resources such as Purdue Owl for guidance because there is no citation generator. There are options to download and print material in the top right corner after selecting an archival material. This can be helpful if you need to highlight or make notes. Be sure to remember the importance of citing when using materials. More information about copyright can be found in the copyright section of this research guide. In addition to scrolling through pdfs, you can use arrows on either side of the item as well as inside of the pdf. This allows you to peruse the item as if you are holding it physically. You can also scroll through pages without opening the pdf on the right side of the item before you open the pdfs. When interacting with audiovisual content, you may be able to access closed captions/subtitles and a transcription. Closed captions (CC) are the written form/transcript of spoken words. You can enable and disable closed captions using the icon below the video - it is the fifth icon from the right. If the icon is white, closed captions are activated; if it is black, closed captions are deactivated. Another icon that will prove useful is the third right icon, which takes the look of a paper with lines on it. This is the "transcript" button, and clicking it brings up the time-stamped transcript of the video. This transcript moves along with the speaker. Using Facets HBCU Digital Collection Research Guide: Conducting Research 2

title: HBCU Digital Collection Research Guide: Conducting Research
text: Facets are located on the left hand side of the CONTENTdm website and can be used to narrow down search results. The facets included are: Collections: helpful in situations in which you want to delve into a particular school or location, and in cases where the beginnings of your research you have determined. Subject: subject headings are explained below, and are useful to narrow down into specific topics. Some of our most used subject headings are included as a reference. Author/Creator: these are the contributors of materials Date: this is the date an object was created. Dates can be very specific, down to the exact day, or it can be a circa date. Circa dating happens when there is uncertainty around the exact dating of an object, so the circa date gives the range of time during which the material could have been created. Formats: the formats included on the website are: application/pdf-text documents - this includes printed materials such as books e.g. the Tuskegee University Printed and Published Materials image/jpeg - these are black and white, and color images e.g. the Dr. Mary McLeod Bethune Photographs collection mp4 - these are audio and video files e.g. sermon videos from the American Baptist College Audio and Visual Collection Geographic Location/Coverage: the location the material/object was created in Interesting Materials Black and White Photographs: Photographs are a form of visual documentation, and the HBCU Digital Collection's photographs span from 1889-2010. Catalogues and Bulletins: These materials analyze courses and programs offered at that school. HBCU Digital Collection Research Guide: Conducting Research 3

title: HBCU Digital Collection Research Guide: Conducting Research
text: Oral Histories: Oral history interviews give individuals the opportunity to speak candidly about their lives and experiences. e.g. the Philander Smith University Oral History Collection Student Newsletters: These showcase the student voice through highlighting their publications. Yearbooks: Yearbooks showcase the student experience as well as interesting events and circumstances during the year captured. Terms to Know While Researching Archive - a physical or digital collection of historical records. Advanced Search - to use facets (defined above) to refine and narrow your search. Boolean - a way to narrow down your search using the words: AND, OR, or NOT. Circa - "circa" before a date means the date is the best approximate inference in the range of +/- 10 years Description - the section of metadata that describes the object. Digital Collection - this describes each collection available on CONTENTdm (defined in the Project Processes section). For example, the Bethune-Cookman University Collection. Digital Repository - this describes the HBCU Digital Collection as a whole; it includes all of the work digitized on CONTENTdm. Metadata - This is a set of data that describes an item. It can include things like the dating of the object or the location it was created. Metadata information for CONTENTdm is located below the object once you have clicked on it. Printed and Published Materials - this includes materials that were originally printed for use and publication. Relation - this section of metadata (within "Object Description") links to another relevant object found in a different collection within the digital repository. It points to commonality through resources, topics, people, events, and HBCUs. HBCU Digital Collection Research Guide: Conducting Research 4

title: HBCU Digital Collection Research Guide: Conducting Research
text: Source - this section of metadata (within "Object Description") informs you about where the material originally came from, or provides additional information related to the object. It provides/links to the corresponding repository, library, and digital collections from the contributing institution. Transcriptions/Transcribed Portion - this language signifies that handwritten content is featured in an object; therefore, we have copied it into written form to aid the end user when reading the content Written on verso - you may see this written in the description of some objects, particularly photographs. This phrase refers to the written content on the back of the object and adds context to the item. Supplemental Databases These databases can be used for supplementing the primary source information found within the collection. Ancestry NARA ProQuest Historical Newspapers: Black Newspaper Collection RADAR: Atlanta University Center Repository Schomburg Center for Research in Black Culture, Photographs and Print Division The HistoryMakers Vital records (accessed based on location) Themes within the HBCU Digital Collection Themes can be used for narrowing down a topic/focus of research and interest. These are a list of themes that can be frequently explored within the collection. This is not an exhaustive list of themes and is meant to be used as a guide. Activism Black History Commencement Economy HBCU Digital Collection Research Guide: Conducting Research 5

title: HBCU Digital Collection Research Guide: Conducting Research
text: Fashion Feminism/Misogyny Genealogy Homecoming Local history Military personnel Music Religion Sports Student life Subject Headings Subject headings are useful as a guiding point to understanding what topics and ideas are seen within materials and collections. They are connected to things like themes and subjects. This is a non-exhaustive list of frequently used subject headings in the HBCU Digital Collection. They are also used to narrow down searches within the website. African american athletes African Americans--Education African American students African American teachers African American universities and colleges African American women Buildings and grounds Campus life College presidents College publications College students HBCU Digital Collection Research Guide: Conducting Research 6

title: HBCU Library Alliance - Programs / HBCU-CUL Digitization Initiative
text: Social Media: Featured Projects - Summer Intership Program >> - Building Capacity for... >> - Authenticity Project >> - HBCU Digital Collection >> - National Partnership with... >> - Virtual Justice Project >> State of Libraries at HBCUs - 2011 >> - 2005 >> Leadership Program Funded by a grant from the Andrew W. Mellon Foundation, the Leadership Program aims to provide theoretical and practical instruction to encourage the development of leadership skills within the HBCU library community and on their own campuses. Learn More >> HBCU-CUL Digitization Initiative The HBCU Library Alliance and Cornell University Library collaboration, "Building Collections, Building Services, and Building Sustainability: A Collaborative Model for the HBCU Library Alliance", is a nationwide initiative to prepare HBCU librarians to produce digital collections and to develop an Internet-based, searchable database of HBCU library materials with descriptive information and select digitization of historic pages. Funded by a grant from The Andrew W. Mellon Foundation and managed by the Cornell University Library, this unique project is one of the first in the nation to build a national framework for digitization of collaborative HBCU library cultural heritage materials and addresses a full-range of digitization needs of historically black college and university libraries. These rich digital resources will be developed and permanently maintained at individual HBCU libraries and the HBCU Library Alliance.Questions or Comments? title: Harvard & the Legacy of Slavery Initiative
filename: Harvard & the Legacy of Slavery Initiative.pdf
text: Harvard Spotlight Henry Louis Gates, Jr. Henry Louis Gates, Jr. is one of six advisors on the Harvard & the Legacy of Slavery Initiative’s Advisory Council. An esteemed scholar in African American History and the Alphonse Fletcher University Professor and director of the Hutchins Center for African and African American Research at Harvard University, Gates is also known for his role in creating, hosting, and producing the PBS series, Finding Your Roots. Gates helps guide the Initiative’s efforts to advance descendant research as a member of the Advisory Board for the Initiative’s genealogical research partner, American Ancestors (https://www.americanancestors.org/). (https://legacyofslavery.harvard.edu/2025/11/21/understanding- our-institutional-history/)  Read Gates’ reflections on understanding Harvard’s institutional history (https://legacyofslavery.harvard.edu/harvard- community/#spotlights)  Read more Harvard community spotlights

title: Harvard & the Legacy of Slavery Initiative
filename: Harvard & the Legacy of Slavery Initiative.pdf
text: About Us Our mission is to understand and cast light on Harvard’s legacies of slavery and address enduring consequences through purposeful change. Our work is grounded in Harvard’s educational mission and the recommendations from the Report of the Presidential Committee on Harvard & the Legacy of Slavery’s recommendations (https://legacyofslaveryreport.harvard.edu/report/recommendations-to-the-president- and-fellows-of-harvard-college). (https://legacyofslavery.harvard.edu/about- us/)  Learn more about the Initiative and the people implementing the work

title: Harvard & the Legacy of Slavery Initiative
filename: Harvard & the Legacy of Slavery Initiative.pdf
text: The Report Harvard University began reckoning with our legacy of slavery more than 15 years ago. In April 2022, the Report of the Presidential Committee on Harvard & the Legacy of Slavery was released. This Report details three difficult truths about the University’s direct, financial, and intellectual ties to slavery, along with seven broad recommendations. (https://legacyofslavery.harvard.edu/explore- report/)  Read the Report detailing the University’s ties to slavery

title: Harvard & the Legacy of Slavery Initiative
filename: Harvard & the Legacy of Slavery Initiative.pdf
text: Implementation Harvard University takes responsibility for our legacy of slavery and seeks to address enduring consequences by partnering with others in the Harvard community and with members of the descendant community in Cambridge, Boston, and beyond. We recognize that this work takes time. Over the next few years, the Initiative will focus our efforts on three priority areas: memorialization (https://legacyofslavery.harvard.edu/memorial-project/), advancing HBCU partnerships (https://legacyofslavery.harvard.edu/hbcu-initiatives/), and supporting descendant communities. (https://legacyofslavery.harvard.edu/supporting- descendants/) We will also continue to provide learning opportunities (https://legacyofslavery.harvard.edu/learning-opportunities/) and invite the Harvard community (https://legacyofslavery.harvard.edu/harvard-community/) to join our reparative work.
